pathophysiology_summary: Allergic contact dermatitis is a delayed-type (type IV) hypersensitivity to an external allergen (here nickel from a belt buckle), producing a pruritic, vesicular, weeping eczematous eruption sharply confined to and geometrically shaped by the contact area, developing over 24–72h. It is benign and resolves with allergen removal and a topical corticosteroid. The ground-truth tasks are to distinguish it from (1) herpes zoster (a dermatomal band not crossing the midline, with neuropathic prodromal pain), (2) impetigo (honey-crusting, painful, spreading, not geometric), and (3) atopic/other eczema (chronic, flexural, no single exposure), and to screen secondary bacterial infection and (very unlikely here) systemic hypersensitivity. Management is nickel avoidance (barrier the buckle), a topical corticosteroid to the affected area, and not popping blisters.

differential.evidence_basis: Geometric vesicular weeping plaque mapped to a new metal buckle in someone who reacts to cheap jewellery.
Vesicular eruption — but zoster is a dermatomal band with neuropathic prodrome, not a geometric contact shape.
Honey-crusting/painful spread would suggest impetigo; not the pattern here.
Acute onset mapped to a single new exposure in a non-flexural site argues against endogenous atopic eczema.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Allergic contact dermatitis leads; herpes zoster and secondary infection must be considered/excluded before topical management.

differential.evidence_basis: Geometric contact-mapped distribution, no dermatomal band/neuropathic prodrome, no honey-crusting/painful spread/fever — allergic contact dermatitis.
Non-dermatomal geometric shape without neuropathic prodrome excludes zoster.
No honey-crusting/painful spread/fever; escalation target if secondary infection develops.

differential_progression.scoring_note: After a negative zoster/infection screen, allergic contact dermatitis leads; allergen avoidance + topical corticosteroid, with an infection safety-net.

differential.evidence_basis: Working diagnosis; resolves with allergen avoidance and TCS; patch testing later if recurrent.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Final position is benign allergic contact dermatitis.

red_flags.description: Secondary bacterial infection / systemic reaction — spreading painful erythema beyond the contact area, honey-crusting/pustules, fever; or whole-body hives/breathing difficulty
red_flags.clinical_significance: Secondary infection needs antibiotics; systemic hypersensitivity needs urgent care.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Calling a geometric contact-mapped vesicular rash 'zoster' and prescribing antivirals — the distribution is not dermatomal.
Treating the rash without addressing nickel exposure, guaranteeing recurrence.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Close inspection for secondary infection / vesicle morphology
Patch testing to confirm the allergen
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Allergen avoidance (barrier the buckle) + topical corticosteroid; GP/ED if secondary infection or systemic features develop.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Missing zoster or a secondary infection.

guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Identify and avoid the allergen; topical corticosteroid to affected skin; emollients; do not rupture blisters. Patch testing (via dermatology) for recurrent/uncertain cases. Treat secondary infection if present.
